ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for mutton vindaloo; closest defensible match is 'meat curry (food product)' FOODON_03302051.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Meat dishes) used as closest defensible category; no specific vindaloo code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mutton vindaloo; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of mutton vindaloo in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of mutton pieces and potatoes in a spiced vinegar-based sauce, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (with gravy) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports mutton curry with gravy as the closest approved primary source for an Indian mutton curry preparation. Values are adapted to account for the vindaloo-specific additions of vinegar, extra chillies, and potatoes, which modestly increase carbohydrate content and reduce protein density relative to a plain mutton curry. The adaptation follows ICMR-NIN compositional data for mutton curry (with gravy) as the anchor, with potato contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN potato data. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and vegetable curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for meat-based curry preparations and potato GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mutton vindaloo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) meat-based curry dishes in the Sydney GI Database typically fall in the 35–50 range due to high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying; (2) potatoes have a GI of approximately 70–80 when boiled, but their contribution is diluted by the dominant meat and sauce matrix; (3) vinegar (acetic acid) is documented in peer-reviewed literature (Liljeberg & Bjorck, 1998, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition) to reduce postprandial glycaemia, further lowering the effective GI of the dish. The composite estimate of GI 45 is conservative and defensible. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 23.8) / 100 = 10.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 6.8 × 3.5 = 23.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mutton vindaloo to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from potatoes, partially offset by vinegar-mediated glycaemic attenuation and high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton vindaloo has a low estimated glycaemic index (~45) due to its high protein and fat content and the presence of vinegar, but the carbohydrate contribution from potatoes (~24 g per serving) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice or bread to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton (~3.4 g per 100 g; ~11.9 g per serving) and high sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume mutton vindaloo in moderation, as the saturated fat content from mutton and the high sodium level in a restaurant serving may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; choosing smaller portions and requesting less oil during preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Vindaloo is a bold, intensely spiced Indian curry originating from Goa, with strong influences from Portuguese colonial cuisine. The dish is characterised by its sharp, tangy, and fiery flavour profile, derived from a marinade and sauce base of red chillies, vinegar (traditionally palm or cane vinegar), garlic, ginger, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and mustard seeds. In Singapore's North Indian restaurant context, mutton (bone-in or boneless goat or sheep meat) is slow-cooked in this pungent sauce together with potatoes, which absorb the spiced gravy and add body to the dish. The result is a deeply flavoured, rich curry with tender meat and soft potatoes in a dark, oil-slicked, tangy sauce. Per 100 g, mutton vindaloo provides approximately 148 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 9.2 g fat (of which 3.4 g is saturated), and 6.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of iron (2.1 mg per 100 g), zinc (2.8 mg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), and vitamin B6 (0.18 mg), reflecting the nutritional profile of red meat. Sodium content is high at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, making a restaurant serving a significant sodium load. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), as the high protein and fat content and the presence of acetic acid from vinegar attenuate postprandial glucose response despite the potato component. Clinically, mutton vindaloo is of moderate concern for individuals managing hypertension (due to sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (due to carbohydrate from potatoes, though GI is low), and cardiovascular disease (due to saturated fat and sodium). In Singapore, it is served at North Indian and Goan-style restaurants and is commonly paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is halal-certified at most Singapore establishments and is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton vindaloo?
What is the glycaemic index of mutton vindaloo?
Difference between vindaloo and other curries
Is vindaloo Goan or North Indian?

confidence_reason: No exact match for mutton vindaloo exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source as a standalone dish entry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (with gravy) data, which is the closest approved primary source for an Indian mutton curry preparation. The adaptation accounts for potato contribution and vinegar addition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database meat curry entries and peer-reviewed evidence on vinegar's glycaemic-attenuating effect. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (with gravy) as the primary anchor, with potato carbohydrate contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN potato compositional data. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mutton vindaloo was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Mutton Vindaloo (Chef Recommendation) (Spicy)' to the canonical English name 'Mutton Vindaloo'; promotional and menu-style qualifiers have been removed per Q1 requirements. 7. Halal status is listed as true based on the North Indian restaurant context in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. The dish is classified under North Indian / Goan cuisine as vindaloo has Goan origins but is widely served across North Indian restaurant menus in Singapore. 9. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from ICMR-NIN fatty acid composition data for mutton and cooking oil used in curry preparation. 10. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant meat (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid), not industrial trans fats.
